agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "A global logistics platform is adding a driver-facing earnings transparency feature across three jurisdictions (California, Germany, Indonesia) with fundamentally different legal classifications (employee vs. independent contractor), regulatory requirements (mandatory transparency vs. works council gatekeeping vs. no requirement), and labor law frameworks. This is not a simple UI feature — it is a jurisdiction-sensitive compensation disclosure system with legal compliance implications.",
    "bounded_context": "Driver Compensation & Earnings Transparency",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Base Pay", "definition": "The fixed compensation a driver receives for being available or completing a delivery, independent of tips or bonuses. In employee jurisdictions (CA), this must meet minimum wage guarantees." },
      { "term": "Per-Delivery Bonus", "definition": "Variable compensation tied to each completed delivery, calculated based on platform-defined criteria (distance, time, demand surge, etc.)." },
      { "term": "Tips", "definition": "Voluntary gratuities from customers passed through to the driver. In CA under AB5, tips must never count toward minimum wage obligations." },
      { "term": "Deductions", "definition": "Amounts subtracted from gross earnings. The nature of deductions varies by classification: for employees (CA), these include taxes, benefits, legally mandated withholdings; for contractors (Indonesia), these may include platform fees, equipment costs, or advances." },
      { "term": "Earnings Breakdown", "definition": "An itemized, per-period view of all compensation components and deductions for a driver, forming the core display of this feature." },
      { "term": "Works Council (Betriebsrat)", "definition": "A legally mandated employee representation body in Germany with co-determination rights. Changes to systems that display or process compensation data require Betriebsrat approval before deployment." },
      { "term": "AB5 Classification", "definition": "California law (Assembly Bill 5) that classifies most gig workers as employees, triggering wage, hour, and transparency requirements." },
      { "term": "Real-Time Earnings", "definition": "Earnings data displayed with minimal delay after each delivery event. Ambiguous — could mean per-delivery update, per-shift running total, or sub-minute refresh." },
      { "term": "Driver Classification", "definition": "The legal designation of a driver as either an employee or independent contractor, which fundamentally determines applicable labor law, tax withholding, and disclosure obligations." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Each jurisdiction must have its own earnings breakdown composition, reflecting the driver's legal classification and local regulatory requirements.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Displaying employee-style deductions to a contractor (or vice versa) creates legal liability — misrepresentation of employment relationship.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a driver in jurisdiction X, the earnings breakdown components shown must match the legally required and applicable categories for that jurisdiction's driver classification."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "In California, the earnings breakdown MUST be displayed to all drivers, including base pay, per-delivery bonus, tips, and all deductions (tax withholdings, benefit contributions, etc.). This is not optional.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "California wage transparency law and AB5 employee classification legally mandate earnings disclosure. Non-compliance exposes the platform to regulatory penalties.",
      "testable_assertion": "Every CA-based driver can view a complete earnings breakdown at any time, with no option for the platform to hide or omit any compensation component."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "In California, tips must be displayed as a separate line item and must never be included in or offset against base pay calculations.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Under AB5 and California Labor Code, tips are the property of the employee and cannot count toward minimum wage. Commingling tips with base pay is a labor law violation.",
      "testable_assertion": "The CA earnings breakdown always shows tips on a distinct line, and the base pay amount is identical whether or not any tips were received."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "In Germany, the earnings transparency feature MUST NOT be deployed to drivers until Betriebsrat (works council) approval has been obtained and documented.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "German co-determination law (Betriebsverfassungsgesetz) gives works councils veto power over systems that monitor or display employee performance and compensation data. Deploying without approval violates German labor law.",
      "testable_assertion": "The feature is gated behind a jurisdiction-level feature flag for Germany, and no German driver can access earnings breakdown until the Betriebsrat approval flag is set to true."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "In Indonesia, the earnings breakdown should display base pay, per-delivery bonus, tips, and platform-related deductions (e.g., platform fees), but NOT employee-style tax withholdings, since drivers are independent contractors.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "No legal mandate for this display in Indonesia — this is a platform policy decision for competitive advantage and driver retention. The composition could be adjusted by business leadership.",
      "testable_assertion": "Indonesian driver earnings breakdown shows contractor-appropriate categories and does not display items implying an employment relationship (e.g., income tax withholding, employer benefit contributions)."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "Deductions displayed must be jurisdiction-specific and classification-appropriate. Employee deductions (tax withholding, social insurance) must only appear for employee-classified drivers; contractor deductions (platform fees, service charges) must only appear for contractor-classified drivers.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Displaying the wrong category of deductions misrepresents the employment relationship and creates legal exposure in both directions — implying employment for contractors or hiding obligations from employees.",
      "testable_assertion": "A contractor-classified driver never sees 'income tax withholding' or 'employer health contribution' in their breakdown; an employee-classified driver never sees 'platform service fee' as a deduction."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "Earnings data must reflect completed, confirmed delivery events only — not pending, disputed, or canceled deliveries.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Showing unconfirmed earnings as realized income is financially misleading and could constitute wage misrepresentation in employee jurisdictions.",
      "testable_assertion": "Only deliveries with a terminal confirmed status contribute to the displayed earnings totals."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [
    {
      "id": "AC-1",
      "given": "A driver registered in California completes a delivery",
      "when": "The driver views their earnings breakdown",
      "then": "They see separate line items for base pay, per-delivery bonus, tips, and all applicable employee deductions (taxes, benefits), with tips shown independently from base pay"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-2",
      "given": "The German Betriebsrat has NOT approved the earnings transparency feature",
      "when": "A German driver attempts to access the earnings breakdown",
      "then": "The feature is unavailable and the driver sees an appropriate message explaining the feature is not yet active in their region"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-3",
      "given": "The German Betriebsrat HAS approved the earnings transparency feature",
      "when": "A German driver views their earnings breakdown",
      "then": "They see a breakdown with components approved by the works council, reflecting German employee classification"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-4",
      "given": "A driver registered in Indonesia completes a delivery",
      "when": "The driver views their earnings breakdown",
      "then": "They see base pay, per-delivery bonus, tips, and contractor-appropriate deductions (platform fees), with no employee-style tax withholdings shown"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-5",
      "given": "A driver has completed three deliveries and one delivery is still in 'disputed' status",
      "when": "The driver views their earnings breakdown",
      "then": "Only the three completed deliveries contribute to the totals; the disputed delivery is excluded or shown separately with a clear status indicator"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-6",
      "given": "A driver in any jurisdiction views their earnings breakdown",
      "when": "The breakdown is rendered",
      "then": "The sum of all displayed components (base pay + bonuses + tips - deductions) equals the displayed net earnings total, with zero discrepancy"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-7",
      "given": "A California driver completes a delivery and receives a tip",
      "when": "The earnings breakdown is calculated",
      "then": "The base pay value is identical to what it would be without the tip — tips do not reduce or offset base pay"
    }
  ],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "A driver transfers from Indonesia (contractor) to California (employee)", "expected_behavior": "Historical earnings retain their original classification-appropriate breakdown. New earnings post-transfer use California employee categories. The transition date is clearly demarcated." },
    { "scenario": "A delivery is completed but the tip is added by the customer hours later", "expected_behavior": "The earnings breakdown updates to include the tip when confirmed, with a clear indication that the total has changed. The base pay must remain unchanged." },
    { "scenario": "A German driver accesses the feature immediately after Betriebsrat approval is granted", "expected_behavior": "Feature becomes available only after the approval flag is propagated system-wide. No partial rollout that could show incomplete data." },
    { "scenario": "A California driver's earnings for a shift fall below minimum wage after deductions", "expected_behavior": "The system must surface this clearly — the platform has a legal obligation to ensure minimum wage compliance under AB5. This may trigger a top-up adjustment visible in the breakdown." },
    { "scenario": "Currency differences across jurisdictions", "expected_behavior": "California shows USD, Germany shows EUR, Indonesia shows IDR. All earnings and deductions are displayed in the driver's local jurisdiction currency." },
    { "scenario": "Platform outage causes delay in earnings data availability", "expected_behavior": "The system must clearly indicate that data is stale or unavailable rather than showing a zero or partial balance, which could be mistaken for actual earnings." },
    { "scenario": "A deduction is applied retroactively (e.g., corrected tax withholding)", "expected_behavior": "The retroactive adjustment appears as a distinct, dated line item — never silently modifies historical breakdowns that the driver may have already relied upon." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Significant compliance cost variation by jurisdiction. California non-compliance risks penalties under AB5 and wage transparency laws (potential class-action exposure). Germany delays are expected but must be budgeted. Indonesia rollout is lowest cost but highest competitive differentiation value for driver acquisition.",
    "operational": "Requires jurisdiction-aware feature flagging infrastructure, per-jurisdiction earnings composition rules, and a works council approval workflow for Germany. Ongoing maintenance burden includes tracking regulatory changes across three legal frameworks.",
    "user": "Drivers gain trust and agency through visible earnings data. In CA, this is legally expected. In Indonesia, this differentiates the platform from competitors. In Germany, delayed rollout may frustrate drivers who learn the feature exists elsewhere.",
    "risk": "HIGH in California (legal non-compliance), MEDIUM in Germany (works council violation), LOW in Indonesia (no legal mandate). Cross-jurisdiction data leakage (showing wrong deduction types) is the highest-severity domain risk.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Drivers (California)", "impact": "Direct beneficiaries — legally entitled to this information. Feature is a compliance requirement, not an enhancement." },
      { "group": "Drivers (Germany)", "impact": "Feature delayed until Betriebsrat approval. Must be communicated transparently to avoid perception of unequal treatment." },
      { "group": "Drivers (Indonesia)", "impact": "Receive a new feature that builds trust and may reduce churn. No legal obligation but strong retention value." },
      { "group": "Legal/Compliance Team", "impact": "Must validate breakdown components per jurisdiction. Ongoing obligation to update as laws change." },
      { "group": "German Works Council (Betriebsrat)", "impact": "Must be formally engaged before any development targeting German drivers. Their requirements may alter the feature's data composition for Germany." },
      { "group": "Finance/Payroll", "impact": "Must ensure earnings breakdown data is consistent with actual payroll/payment records. Discrepancies create audit and legal risk." },
      { "group": "Product/Engineering", "impact": "Must build jurisdiction-aware, classification-aware earnings composition — not a single global template." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 52,
    "reasoning": "The spec provides clear jurisdiction and classification context but leaves several critical implementation details ambiguous: the definition of 'real time,' the specific deduction categories per jurisdiction, the handling of the Germany pre-approval period, and whether Indonesia's feature is opt-in or default. These ambiguities prevent full confidence in the acceptance criteria.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "California AB5 employee classification requires mandatory wage transparency",
      "Tips must be separate from base pay in California",
      "Germany requires Betriebsrat approval before deployment — hard gate",
      "Indonesia drivers are contractors — different deduction categories apply",
      "Jurisdiction-specific earnings composition is a non-negotiable invariant"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Exact deduction line items per jurisdiction are not enumerated in the spec",
      "Whether the Betriebsrat may require modifications to the breakdown format (not just approve/reject)",
      "Minimum wage top-up display requirements under AB5 — needs legal review",
      "Data retention and audit trail requirements per jurisdiction"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "The platform already has jurisdiction and driver classification data available in its domain model",
      "Earnings data flows from a delivery completion event and is the system of record",
      "The works council process in Germany is binary (approved/not approved) rather than iterative with modification requests",
      "Currency is determined by jurisdiction, not driver preference"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "'Real time' is undefined. Does it mean per-delivery instant update, per-shift running total, sub-minute polling, or end-of-day batch? Each interpretation has different infrastructure costs and user experience implications. In California, delayed display could be construed as a transparency violation.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers (all jurisdictions)", "Engineering", "Legal/Compliance"],
      "options": ["Per-delivery event-driven update (true real-time)", "Running total refreshed every few minutes", "End-of-shift summary with intra-shift estimates"],
      "recommendation": "Per-delivery event-driven update for California (legal safety), with jurisdiction-configurable refresh cadence elsewhere. Define 'real time' explicitly in the spec as 'within 60 seconds of delivery confirmation.'"
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "'Deductions' is vaguely specified. For employees (CA, DE), deductions could include federal/state taxes, social insurance, health benefits, union dues, garnishments, etc. For contractors (ID), deductions could mean platform commission, equipment rental, insurance opt-in, cash advances. The spec does not enumerate which deductions to display per jurisdiction.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers (all jurisdictions)", "Finance/Payroll", "Legal/Compliance"],
      "options": ["Display all deductions from payroll/payment system", "Display only legally required deduction categories", "Display a curated, jurisdiction-specific set defined by product/legal"],
      "recommendation": "Legal and finance teams must define the exact deduction taxonomy per jurisdiction before development begins. This is a domain decision, not an engineering decision."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The Germany rollout constraint says Betriebsrat 'must approve changes to compensation display systems before rollout,' but does not specify: (a) whether the works council can request modifications to the breakdown format, (b) what the platform should show German drivers in the interim, (c) whether existing compensation views must also be frozen pending approval.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers (Germany)", "German Works Council", "Legal/Compliance", "Product"],
      "options": ["Show nothing until approval (full gate)", "Show a simplified, pre-existing earnings view until approval", "Engage Betriebsrat early in design to co-create the German version"],
      "recommendation": "Engage the Betriebsrat during the design phase, not after. Their input may reshape the German feature, and early engagement reduces approval timeline risk."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}

content.thinking:
Let me analyze this logistics platform "driver earnings transparency" feature across three jurisdictions with different legal frameworks.

This is a complex domain scenario with significant regulatory/legal considerations. Let me classify the primary blocker.

The scenario describes requirements across three jurisdictions with different legal frameworks. The key issue is that there are significant missing pieces:
- What are the specific AB5 compliance requirements for earnings display?
- What is the works council approval status in Germany?
- What deductions are applicable in each jurisdiction?
- What does "real time" mean exactly?

Actually, looking more carefully:
- California: wage transparency legally required + AB5 employee classification → clear rules exist


- Germany: works council must approve before rollout → this is a hard constraint
- Indonesia: no legal requirement → policy decision

The core problem is ambiguity across multiple dimensions. "Real time" and "deductions" are both underspecified in the requirements, and there's no clarity on how to handle Germany's pre-approval state or what the feature should display during that period. These vague terms create multiple valid interpretations rather than a single clear path forward.

The scenario mentions AB5 requirements but doesn't spell out the specific display obligations that come with it. Combined with the four key ambiguities—what "real time" actually means, which deductions apply by jurisdiction, what the feature does before works council approval in Germany, and whether Indonesia's feature is optional or mandatory—this points to ambiguity rather than missing context. The requirements exist but lack the specificity needed to implement them consistently.

This is an ambiguity escalation with moderate confidence around 55%.

    },

trace.result:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "A global logistics platform is adding a driver-facing earnings transparency feature across three jurisdictions (California, Germany, Indonesia) with fundamentally different legal classifications (employee vs. independent contractor), regulatory requirements (mandatory transparency vs. works council gatekeeping vs. no requirement), and labor law frameworks. This is not a simple UI feature — it is a jurisdiction-sensitive compensation disclosure system with legal compliance implications.",
    "bounded_context": "Driver Compensation & Earnings Transparency",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Base Pay", "definition": "The fixed compensation a driver receives for being available or completing a delivery, independent of tips or bonuses. In employee jurisdictions (CA), this must meet minimum wage guarantees." },
      { "term": "Per-Delivery Bonus", "definition": "Variable compensation tied to each completed delivery, calculated based on platform-defined criteria (distance, time, demand surge, etc.)." },
      { "term": "Tips", "definition": "Voluntary gratuities from customers passed through to the driver. In CA under AB5, tips must never count toward minimum wage obligations." },
      { "term": "Deductions", "definition": "Amounts subtracted from gross earnings. The nature of deductions varies by classification: for employees (CA), these include taxes, benefits, legally mandated withholdings; for contractors (Indonesia), these may include platform fees, equipment costs, or advances." },
      { "term": "Earnings Breakdown", "definition": "An itemized, per-period view of all compensation components and deductions for a driver, forming the core display of this feature." },
      { "term": "Works Council (Betriebsrat)", "definition": "A legally mandated employee representation body in Germany with co-determination rights. Changes to systems that display or process compensation data require Betriebsrat approval before deployment." },
      { "term": "AB5 Classification", "definition": "California law (Assembly Bill 5) that classifies most gig workers as employees, triggering wage, hour, and transparency requirements." },
      { "term": "Real-Time Earnings", "definition": "Earnings data displayed with minimal delay after each delivery event. Ambiguous — could mean per-delivery update, per-shift running total, or sub-minute refresh." },
      { "term": "Driver Classification", "definition": "The legal designation of a driver as either an employee or independent contractor, which fundamentally determines applicable labor law, tax withholding, and disclosure obligations." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Each jurisdiction must have its own earnings breakdown composition, reflecting the driver's legal classification and local regulatory requirements.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Displaying employee-style deductions to a contractor (or vice versa) creates legal liability — misrepresentation of employment relationship.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a driver in jurisdiction X, the earnings breakdown components shown must match the legally required and applicable categories for that jurisdiction's driver classification."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "In California, the earnings breakdown MUST be displayed to all drivers, including base pay, per-delivery bonus, tips, and all deductions (tax withholdings, benefit contributions, etc.). This is not optional.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "California wage transparency law and AB5 employee classification legally mandate earnings disclosure. Non-compliance exposes the platform to regulatory penalties.",
      "testable_assertion": "Every CA-based driver can view a complete earnings breakdown at any time, with no option for the platform to hide or omit any compensation component."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "In California, tips must be displayed as a separate line item and must never be included in or offset against base pay calculations.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Under AB5 and California Labor Code, tips are the property of the employee and cannot count toward minimum wage. Commingling tips with base pay is a labor law violation.",
      "testable_assertion": "The CA earnings breakdown always shows tips on a distinct line, and the base pay amount is identical whether or not any tips were received."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "In Germany, the earnings transparency feature MUST NOT be deployed to drivers until Betriebsrat (works council) approval has been obtained and documented.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "German co-determination law (Betriebsverfassungsgesetz) gives works councils veto power over systems that monitor or display employee performance and compensation data. Deploying without approval violates German labor law.",
      "testable_assertion": "The feature is gated behind a jurisdiction-level feature flag for Germany, and no German driver can access earnings breakdown until the Betriebsrat approval flag is set to true."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "In Indonesia, the earnings breakdown should display base pay, per-delivery bonus, tips, and platform-related deductions (e.g., platform fees), but NOT employee-style tax withholdings, since drivers are independent contractors.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "No legal mandate for this display in Indonesia — this is a platform policy decision for competitive advantage and driver retention. The composition could be adjusted by business leadership.",
      "testable_assertion": "Indonesian driver earnings breakdown shows contractor-appropriate categories and does not display items implying an employment relationship (e.g., income tax withholding, employer benefit contributions)."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "Deductions displayed must be jurisdiction-specific and classification-appropriate. Employee deductions (tax withholding, social insurance) must only appear for employee-classified drivers; contractor deductions (platform fees, service charges) must only appear for contractor-classified drivers.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Displaying the wrong category of deductions misrepresents the employment relationship and creates legal exposure in both directions — implying employment for contractors or hiding obligations from employees.",
      "testable_assertion": "A contractor-classified driver never sees 'income tax withholding' or 'employer health contribution' in their breakdown; an employee-classified driver never sees 'platform service fee' as a deduction."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "Earnings data must reflect completed, confirmed delivery events only — not pending, disputed, or canceled deliveries.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Showing unconfirmed earnings as realized income is financially misleading and could constitute wage misrepresentation in employee jurisdictions.",
      "testable_assertion": "Only deliveries with a terminal confirmed status contribute to the displayed earnings totals."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [
    {
      "id": "AC-1",
      "given": "A driver registered in California completes a delivery",
      "when": "The driver views their earnings breakdown",
      "then": "They see separate line items for base pay, per-delivery bonus, tips, and all applicable employee deductions (taxes, benefits), with tips shown independently from base pay"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-2",
      "given": "The German Betriebsrat has NOT approved the earnings transparency feature",
      "when": "A German driver attempts to access the earnings breakdown",
      "then": "The feature is unavailable and the driver sees an appropriate message explaining the feature is not yet active in their region"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-3",
      "given": "The German Betriebsrat HAS approved the earnings transparency feature",
      "when": "A German driver views their earnings breakdown",
      "then": "They see a breakdown with components approved by the works council, reflecting German employee classification"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-4",
      "given": "A driver registered in Indonesia completes a delivery",
      "when": "The driver views their earnings breakdown",
      "then": "They see base pay, per-delivery bonus, tips, and contractor-appropriate deductions (platform fees), with no employee-style tax withholdings shown"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-5",
      "given": "A driver has completed three deliveries and one delivery is still in 'disputed' status",
      "when": "The driver views their earnings breakdown",
      "then": "Only the three completed deliveries contribute to the totals; the disputed delivery is excluded or shown separately with a clear status indicator"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-6",
      "given": "A driver in any jurisdiction views their earnings breakdown",
      "when": "The breakdown is rendered",
      "then": "The sum of all displayed components (base pay + bonuses + tips - deductions) equals the displayed net earnings total, with zero discrepancy"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-7",
      "given": "A California driver completes a delivery and receives a tip",
      "when": "The earnings breakdown is calculated",
      "then": "The base pay value is identical to what it would be without the tip — tips do not reduce or offset base pay"
    }
  ],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "A driver transfers from Indonesia (contractor) to California (employee)", "expected_behavior": "Historical earnings retain their original classification-appropriate breakdown. New earnings post-transfer use California employee categories. The transition date is clearly demarcated." },
    { "scenario": "A delivery is completed but the tip is added by the customer hours later", "expected_behavior": "The earnings breakdown updates to include the tip when confirmed, with a clear indication that the total has changed. The base pay must remain unchanged." },
    { "scenario": "A German driver accesses the feature immediately after Betriebsrat approval is granted", "expected_behavior": "Feature becomes available only after the approval flag is propagated system-wide. No partial rollout that could show incomplete data." },
    { "scenario": "A California driver's earnings for a shift fall below minimum wage after deductions", "expected_behavior": "The system must surface this clearly — the platform has a legal obligation to ensure minimum wage compliance under AB5. This may trigger a top-up adjustment visible in the breakdown." },
    { "scenario": "Currency differences across jurisdictions", "expected_behavior": "California shows USD, Germany shows EUR, Indonesia shows IDR. All earnings and deductions are displayed in the driver's local jurisdiction currency." },
    { "scenario": "Platform outage causes delay in earnings data availability", "expected_behavior": "The system must clearly indicate that data is stale or unavailable rather than showing a zero or partial balance, which could be mistaken for actual earnings." },
    { "scenario": "A deduction is applied retroactively (e.g., corrected tax withholding)", "expected_behavior": "The retroactive adjustment appears as a distinct, dated line item — never silently modifies historical breakdowns that the driver may have already relied upon." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Significant compliance cost variation by jurisdiction. California non-compliance risks penalties under AB5 and wage transparency laws (potential class-action exposure). Germany delays are expected but must be budgeted. Indonesia rollout is lowest cost but highest competitive differentiation value for driver acquisition.",
    "operational": "Requires jurisdiction-aware feature flagging infrastructure, per-jurisdiction earnings composition rules, and a works council approval workflow for Germany. Ongoing maintenance burden includes tracking regulatory changes across three legal frameworks.",
    "user": "Drivers gain trust and agency through visible earnings data. In CA, this is legally expected. In Indonesia, this differentiates the platform from competitors. In Germany, delayed rollout may frustrate drivers who learn the feature exists elsewhere.",
    "risk": "HIGH in California (legal non-compliance), MEDIUM in Germany (works council violation), LOW in Indonesia (no legal mandate). Cross-jurisdiction data leakage (showing wrong deduction types) is the highest-severity domain risk.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Drivers (California)", "impact": "Direct beneficiaries — legally entitled to this information. Feature is a compliance requirement, not an enhancement." },
      { "group": "Drivers (Germany)", "impact": "Feature delayed until Betriebsrat approval. Must be communicated transparently to avoid perception of unequal treatment." },
      { "group": "Drivers (Indonesia)", "impact": "Receive a new feature that builds trust and may reduce churn. No legal obligation but strong retention value." },
      { "group": "Legal/Compliance Team", "impact": "Must validate breakdown components per jurisdiction. Ongoing obligation to update as laws change." },
      { "group": "German Works Council (Betriebsrat)", "impact": "Must be formally engaged before any development targeting German drivers. Their requirements may alter the feature's data composition for Germany." },
      { "group": "Finance/Payroll", "impact": "Must ensure earnings breakdown data is consistent with actual payroll/payment records. Discrepancies create audit and legal risk." },
      { "group": "Product/Engineering", "impact": "Must build jurisdiction-aware, classification-aware earnings composition — not a single global template." }
    ]
  },

    ],
    "assumptions": [

The spec does not enumerate which deductions to display per jurisdiction.",
